posts.caption:
It is not hard to imagine the pull Larry Stanton had on the men at Fire Island Pines.⁠
⁠
Best known for his pretty-boy portraits and pencil drawings, Stanton is revisited in Larry Stanton: Friends + Lovers, a new exhibition opening soon at @vstrscenter, through his photographs and films. “I’ve always thought of Larry’s photographs and drawings as two chapters of the same story. The camera allowed him to collect encounters. He photographed people he met in the streets [which] often became the starting point for his portraits,” co-curator @fabio_cherstich says. “The drawings are never simple translations of photographs. They’re acts of remembrance. Larry wasn’t interested in documentary realism […] he was searching for the emotional truth of a face.”⁠
⁠
Perhaps more so than his drawings, Stanton’s photographs have a conversational register. The washed-out images record not just the subject but the conditions of exchange. Stanton’s subjects are seduced by him, and he by them. ⁠
⁠
@w.lliam speaks with the show's curators Cherstich and @michaeljbullock to explore the exhibition at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Larry Stanton: Friends + Lovers. Photography by #LarryStanton, 1975. @larry_stanton_art

posts.caption:
“I never kept a teenage diary,” writes @clairemahealy in her latest @girlhoodstudies column. Growing up online in the mid-2000s, she was more likely to pour her anxieties into Sufjan Stevens forums, MSN Messenger screenshots or “various photographs of fashion campaigns I collected into a messy hard drive”. But, looking back, even these fragments of a former self seem to have something public about them.⁠
⁠
For AnOther, Healy revisits five books which transform the memories of girlhood into literature, exploring the strange unreality of encountering a younger self through writing. “The magic of these texts occurs in applying a size, shape and structure to the memories of one’s coming-of-age,” she writes. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Lead image: Mémoire de fille, 2026 (Film still)

posts.caption:
On 9 July, @mariagraziachiuri debuted at the @fendi AW26 Couture show at Galleria Nazionale d’Arte Moderna e Contemporanea in Rome. Chiuri reinterpreted the 1985 exhibition “After un percorso di lavoro. Fendi/Karl Lagerfeld 1985,” which was conceived to mark Karl Lagerfeld’s 20th anniversary with FENDI. Beginning with the words, “I believe the Labyrinth is a metaphor of the creative process,” Chiuri looked to FENDI’s heritage not to replicate it, but to reinterpret it in her own way.
Rather than oversized silhouettes that transformed the body through exaggerated proportions, Chiuri’s collection focused on A-line silhouettes that highlighted the natural shape of the female form. Instead of imposing an artificial structure on the body, the collection allowed natural movement and the body’s own form to define the silhouette.
Beyond presenting a new vision for FENDI, Chiuri introduced a new expression of beauty to the world of Haute Couture—one that moves beyond exaggerated and theatrical designs.
Swipe right to see more looks from the collection.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now
📷 Paolo Lanzi (Gorunway.com)
#Fendi #MariaGraziaChiuri
#HauteCouture #AW26
posts.alt_text:
Jean Paul Gaultier trained under Pierre Cardin, then spent the next five decades doing the opposite of what Cardin taught him. Corsets became outerwear. Sailor stripes became a personal uniform — he’s said to own more than 300 versions of the same striped shirt. Men wore skirts on his runway in 1984, long before anyone had a term for what that meant.

He made Madonna’s cone bra for the Blond Ambition tour. He ran Hermès for seven years without losing his own signature underneath it. He closed his own runway in January 2020 after a 50th-anniversary couture show, then handed the house to a rotating cast of guest designers instead of walking away completely.

Here’s Gaultier, on camera, answering the question people have been asking him his entire career.

Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

🎥 ABC News

#JeanPaulGaultier #HauteCouture #Interview #Archive #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
BREAKING: Hermès is entering haute couture — and the debut is set for January 2027.
The house will stage its first-ever haute couture runway show during Paris Couture Week, marking artistic director Nadège Vanhée’s first foray into the category after over a decade helming women’s ready-to-wear.
Hermès first signalled the move in early 2025, and CEO Axel Dumas has since confirmed the studio has been “beefed up” with a dedicated couture atelier, including new hires like designer Léa Peckre (ex-Céline).
The timing is no accident: Grace Wales Bonner will also make her runway debut as Hermès’ new menswear creative director that same month — meaning the house opens 2027 with two major creative firsts, back to back.
To earn haute couture status, a house must design made-to-order pieces for private clients, run a Paris atelier with a minimum number of artisans, present twice a year, and do the work predominantly by hand. Only a handful of houses currently qualify — Hermès joining that circle is a genuine industry moment.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @ok__mccausland
#Hermes #HauteCouture #NadegeVanhee #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
Duran Lantink just delivered his haute couture debut for Jean Paul Gaultier. Alongside the fairytale gowns you’d expect from couture season, Lantink sent out silhouettes that looked genuinely uncanny — tubular forms growing off the body, shapes chopped up and projected outward, pieces that read more like fever dream hallucinations than couture sketches. Turns out the reference wasn’t futuristic at all: it was 40 years old, pulled from Gaultier’s 1985 costumes for choreographer Régine Chopinot. A newcomer to the couture world, Lantink admitted he’s still learning the atelier and its famously fearless clientele — but he sounds sure they’ll rise to the occasion. Bold debut, bolder question: will the house’s “powerful women” wear it?
What do you think — genius reinvention or a step too far?
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 Vogue Runway
#JeanPaulGaultier #HauteCouture #AW26 #DuranLantink #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
On a hot summer day, a hat is probably one of the first things you’ll want to grab before stepping outside. But finding one that suits you is not always as easy as it sounds. Even the editor spent a long time searching for the right one — until discovering @47europe .
A familiar name in the world of headwear, #47Europe offers a wide range of silhouettes, colours, and designs — from everyday classics to styles that add a subtle statement to any outfit.
Now, Soho is giving hat lovers the chance to explore the brand’s full range in person. Our editor stopped by the pop-up first, tried on different styles, picked a favourite, and even customised it to create a one-of-a-kind piece.
Open until December, the ’47 Pop-Up Store invites you to explore the collection, try on different styles, and find the hat that feels like your own. It could easily become your new everyday go-to.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#LondonPopUp #Soho
#LondonThingsToDo #SummerStyle

posts.caption:
From #TheGreatGatsby’s Jordan Baker to Diana, Princess of Wales, #ElizabethDebicki has inhabited some of popular culture’s most recognisable figures.
In our July cover story, the award-winning actor reflects on finding the humanity behind iconic characters and why every role begins with a blank slate. “But personally, I’m interested in trying to shed that and create as much open space to imagine, as you would with a character that’s entirely your own creation.”
Discover the full interview at the link in our bio.
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout.
Words @tessdevivie
Photography @justinridler
Dp @alicenaish
Styling @jessicasteuarthoyler
Hair @______renyaxydis______
Makeup @victoriabaron
Styling assistant @breepritch.Stylist
On Set Production @aprh_productions
Tailor @sarah__woodall
Production Director @rebeccamooreproductions
Art Director @_cathryn.s.z
Digital Director @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
For #ElizabethDebicki, coming home to Melbourne is always a gift. As our July cover star, the award-winning actor reflects on family, finding meaning in the stories she tells, and why following instinct has become the guiding force of her career.

“As an Australian, we have this unspoken pact that you do not get to hate planes, flights or turbulence if you want to go back home and see the people you love.”

The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout. 

Words @tessdevivie

posts.caption:
Elizabeth Debicki has become one of the most compelling actors of her generation. From Melbourne to Hollywood, our July cover star reflects on inhabiting iconic women, choosing stories with purpose, and why she’s only just getting started.
“My vetting process when I read a script is asking myself, if someone called me up and said you have to go on set next week, how excited am I about doing that?”
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
#ElizabethDebicki wears Panthère de @Cartier necklace in white gold, onyx, set with four emeralds and 1,478 brilliant-cut diamonds, and Panthère de Cartier ring in white gold, set with four emeralds, onyx and 298 brilliant-cut diamonds. @toni_maticevski dress, bodysuit and skirt.

While all eyes are on #pariscoutureweek, one of the world’s most coveted @rolex watches is quietly making headlines much closer to home.
The Rainbow Daytona is heading to @first_state_auctions in Australia for the first time, leading what is believed to be the country’s biggest watch auction. Alongside it? A $2 million private collection featuring rare @richardmille, @audemarspiguet and @rolex timepieces, plus an @hermes Birkin, a 10-carat diamond and more.

posts.alt_text:
From #TheGreatGatsby’s Jordan Baker to Diana, Princess of Wales, #ElizabethDebicki has inhabited some of popular culture’s most recognisable figures. 

In our July cover story, the award-winning actor reflects on finding the humanity behind iconic characters and why every role begins with a blank slate. “But personally, I’m interested in trying to shed that and create as much open space to imagine, as you would with a character that’s entirely your own creation.”

Discover the full interview at the link in our bio. 

The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)

Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk
posts.alt_text:
From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)

posts.caption:
@GucciBeauty has a new home — but the renovations, so to speak, are just getting started.
On Tuesday, L’Oréal confirmed that it would take over Gucci’s beauty licence from Coty in June 2027, one year ahead of its original expiration date, as part of its $4.6 billion deal with parent company Kering to run its beauty business.
It’s positive for both Kering and L’Oréal, though L’Oréal also inherits some baggage. Unlike the other brands it will oversee in beauty as part of the Kering deal, Gucci is well established, but its beauty line has been in the midst of an identity crisis since the 2022 departure of creative director Alessandro Michele. It hasn’t been updated since, and as such, its fashion and beauty outputs no longer connect.
BoF's Daniela Morosini (@danielakmorosini) explores the opportunity — and the challenge — awaiting L’Oréal. Read more #linkinbio
📷 BoF Studio, @guccibeauty

posts.caption:
In recent weeks, as record-breaking heat gripped fashion capitals like Paris, London and New York, the conditions in fashion’s supply chain were even more extreme.
In cities such as Delhi, Dhaka and Hanoi, where garment factories produce apparel for countless US and European brands, temperatures routinely rose above 40 degrees Celsius (104 degrees Fahrenheit), and have at times since April.
For garment workers, the rising temperatures in factories are dangerous, causing dehydration, dizziness, fainting and worse — a fact that by itself should be reason for fashion to act.
BoF's Shayeza Walid (@shayeza_) looks at the practical changes that could protect workers — and production. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
Luxury’s post-pandemic boom is over. Growth has slowed, shoppers are pushing back on years of price increases and many brands are struggling to convince customers they’re still worth the premium. Drawing on BoF’s 2026 State of Luxury report — which includes a survey of more than 2,000 affluent consumers in the US and China, alongside interviews with industry leaders — BoF editors Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) and Robert Williams (@r.o.bertwilliams)  explore what’s driving luxury’s reset and what it means for brands.
They unpack why heritage, craftsmanship and logo power are no longer enough to justify high-ticket prices, and why emotional connection, creativity and exceptional retail experiences have become more important to today’s luxury shopper. They also discuss what brands need to do to rebuild excitement, restore value and win customers back.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
@MariaGraziaChiuri, like most top creative directors, is a creature of habit. She does her thing, with a few adjustments, at whichever house she inhabits. Call it signature, call it stubbornness.
In her first @Fendi couture show, staged Thursday in Rome, her signatures were all accounted for: a flaunted link with the world of the arts — often used as a source of ennobling context — a proclivity for fashions that are rarely too idealised or theatrical (despite her fascination with dance and theatre), but rather streamlined and “real.” Clothes that are flattering, if at times somber. Hers is the position of a practical woman designing for women.
Read Angelo Flaccavento (@poeticallypunk)’s full report on Maria Grazia Chiuri’s Fendi couture debut #linkinbio
📷 Spotlight/Launchmetrics.com

posts.caption:
In “Elle,” Prime Video’s new “Legally Blonde” prequel series, ads are everywhere — even if viewers might not realise it.
When protagonist Elle Woods sits poolside flipping through an issue of Cosmopolitan in the pilot, there’s a glimpse of a 1990s-style ad for a pink version of Dove’s beauty bar, with copy that nodded to the character’s Valley Girl speak: “It’s like... the best for your skin!” Two episodes later, she’s washing her face with the very same product. And during the ad breaks, there’s a commercial starring actress Lexi Minetree, who plays Woods, highlighting the beauty bar once again. What’s more, intrigued shoppers can snap up their own pink beauty bar with just a few clicks of their remote, which adds the product to their Amazon cart.
BoF's Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores how "Elle" is turning traditional product placement into a fully integrated shopping experience. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @lorealparis, @dove, @vspink

posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption:
The intricate techniques can be appreciated if you really pay attention to the details- she wants to make it seem chic and effortless but applying couture techniques to the designs.
Grand and extravagant, no, but she’s not that.
I see her aesthetic working here and may I say has been evolving even more and polished to a next level.
Very elegant. Couture doesn’t have to be an exaggeration rarity circus all the time for all the brands.

posts.caption:
A trained ballet and contemporary dancer, our latest cover star Sanya Malhotra (@sanyamalhotra_) is an “unconventional” actor who pirouetted her way into hearts with numerous relatable roles—and many a viral reel—carving a unique niche for herself in the industry along the way. Brides Today gets a glimpse into her universe.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)
Interview: Rewati Rau (@rewatinair) and Shraddha Chowdhury (@shraddha.skc)
Photographer: Sasha Jairam (@sashajairam)
Creative Direction & Stylist: Anushree Sardesai (@anushree_sardesai)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Make-up and Hair Artist: Natasha Mathias (@natasha_mathias_)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Set Design: Nikita Rao (@nikita_315)
Style Assistant: Lobo (@michellemlobo__)
Artist Reputation Management: Raindrop Media (@media.raindrop)
Sanya is wearing a dark fuschia bridal lehenga set, Safar Collection Osaa by Adarsh (@osaabyadarsh). All jewellery by, Khurana Jewellery House via Amigos (@khuranajewelleryhouse).
#BridesToday #SanyaMalhotra
posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio. 

Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)
Interview: Shraddha Chowdhury (@shraddha.skc)
Photographer: Taras Taraporvala (@taras84)
Creative Direction: Avantikka Kilachand Raju (@avantikkak)
Styling: Yukti Sodha (@yuktisodha)
Assistant Stylist: Anushree Sardesai (@anushree_sardesai)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Hair Artist: Madhav Trehan (@hairstylist_madhav2.0)
Makeup Artist: Niccky Rajaanie (@niccky_rajaanie)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Set Design: Janhavi Patwardhan (@artnut_j)
Artist Reputation Management: Communiqué Film Pr (@communiquefilmpr)

Bhumi is wearing an almond-ivory and maroon lehenga set from the East collection by JJ Valaya (@jjvalaya); Jewellery, maang tikka by Tribe by Amrapali (@tribebyamrapali); bangles by Ritika Sachdeva (@sachdeva.ritika), Tribe by Amrapali, and PC Totuka & Sons (@pctandsons); rings by Golecha’s Jewels (@golecha_jewels) and PC Totuka & Sons; Shoes: Inochhi (@inochhiofficial); Trunk: Courrier Lozine 110 by Louis Vuitton (@louisvuitton).

#BridesTodayIndia #BhumiPednekar

posts.caption:
Sometimes, it’s the smallest details that tell the sweetest love stories. At Anshula Kapoor’s wedding festivities, Janhvi Kapoor (@janhvikapoor) embraced the timeless tradition of hiding her partner, Shikhar Pahariya (@shikharpahariya) name in her mehendi, with fans spotting “Shikhu” woven into her intricate design. From her playful “If lost, please return to Shikhar” T-shirt to this thoughtful mehendi detail, it’s just the latest in a series of subtle yet heartfelt nods to their relationship—and we’re here for every bit of it.
📷: Janhvi Kapoor, @showmo_india @bollywoodfeels
#BridesTodayIn #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
@luxpascal_ is one to watch—and not for long. After a breakout year that included a Netflix film, an Off-Broadway debut in “Richard II,” and a casting announcement in Tom Ford’s next feature film, she’s stepping into a moment that’s been building for years.
For our #RenewalIssue, our cover star reflects on rejection, momentum, and what comes next.
Photography: @quillemons
Styling: @dionemdavis
Styling Assistant: @dylan.keoni
Words: @harnwaw
Hair: @blakeerik
Makeup: @cgonzalezbeauty using @armanibeauty
Props: @carrieashleyh
1st Assistant: @actual_sam_dole
2nd Assistant: @thaliaruina
Executive Creative Producer: @cscotttt @otherhalfcreative
Set Producer:  @lindseymgardner
PA: @maddie_galer
Editor-in-Chief: @faith_xue
Senior Fashion Editor: @ella_okeeffe
Social Strategy Lead: @kalaherh
Social Creative Lead: @tashasheridan
Graphic Designer: @sierraccook
posts.alt_text:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

Photographer: @laaapavi
Makeup: @pircillapae (@aframe_agency)
Hair: @eduardomendezhair (@aframe_agency)
Editor-in-Chief: @faith_xue 
Senior Fashion Editor: @ella_okeeffe 
Social Creative Lead: @tashasheridan 
Social Strategy Lead: @kalaherh
Production: @gillianstern

posts.caption:
Before social media turned style into an endless stream of references, London’s youth subcultures were something far more radical: they were identities.
Throughout the late 1970s and 1980s, punks, goths, mods, teddy boys and New Romantics each built their own identities through clothing, music, clubs and attitude. Inside clubs like Blitz, The Batcave, Taboo and Le Beat Route, fashion became a form of self-expression where rules could be broken, identities reinvented and entire movements born on the dance floor.
Many of these images were captured by the legendary British photographer Derek Ridgers, whose work remains one of the most important visual records of London’s underground. Others come from different photographers who documented the same era, together preserving a generation that transformed youth culture forever.
Looking back, these photographs feel like more than a fashion archive. They capture a time when getting dressed meant belonging to something, and when London’s underground didn’t just influence fashion — it changed it forever !
posts.alt_text:
For the first time in years, Balenciaga felt like Balenciaga again, the Balenciaga Cristóbal once imagined.

Rather than revisiting the house through irony or provocation, Pierpaolo Piccioli looked back to what made Cristóbal Balenciaga extraordinary in the first place: sculptural volume, architectural precision and an unwavering respect for couture as an art form. Yet this wasn’t an exercise in nostalgia. Every look carried Piccioli’s unmistakable signature, his fearless use of colour, his emotional approach to dressing, and his belief that couture should celebrate the person wearing it, not overshadow them.

Throughout the collection, Cristóbal’s philosophy could be felt in the construction rather than the imitation. Coats floated away from the body, gowns were engineered instead of sculpted by corsets, and dramatic proportions never lost their sense of lightness. It echoed the founder’s belief that a couturier should be an architect, a painter and a philosopher all at once.

What made the debut so compelling was its confidence. Piccioli didn’t attempt to recreate Cristóbal Balenciaga, he translated his values through his own language. The result felt both deeply respectful and unmistakably modern, proving that a house can honour its past without becoming trapped by it.

For the first time in a long time, Balenciaga’s couture felt like it was speaking in Cristóbal’s language again.

Collection: Fall 2026 Haute Couture
Creative Director: Pierpaolo Piccioli

posts.caption:
Nobody turned bridalwear into an act of rebellion quite like Vivienne Westwood.
That philosophy reached one of its most unforgettable modern expressions at Vivienne Westwood’s Fall/Winter 2020 show in Paris. Closing the runway, Bella Hadid emerged in a dramatic white lace gown with sculptural sleeves, corsetry, and a dagger tucked into her waist. Half bride, half warrior, a Look that leaves a lot of room for interpretation.
The symbolism ran deeper than the dress itself. Creative Director Andreas Kronthaler built the collection around the idea of confronting darkness and pushing back against negative forces. Garlic garlands, spiritual references, and protective symbols appeared throughout the show, while Bella’s unsheathed dagger became its final statement: strength can be elegant, and beauty can be fearless.
Looking back, the finale feels even more powerful. Bella Hadid had long been one of Vivienne Westwood’s most natural muses, embracing the house’s corsetry, historical references, and rebellious spirit both on and off the runway. That final bridal look captured everything the brand has always stood for: romance without submission, tradition without conformity, and fashion as an act of defiance.
Some runway finales disappear with the season. This one became part of Vivienne Westwood’s legacy.
Collection: Andreas Kronthaler for Vivienne Westwood Fall/Winter 2020 Ready-to-Wear
Creative Director: Andreas Kronthaler
Founder: Vivienne Westwood
Closing Look: Bella Hadid
Venue: Hôtel de Ville, Paris
posts.alt_text:
Before social media turned style into an endless stream of references, London’s youth subcultures were something far more radical: they were identities.

posts.caption:
Some editorials ask you to look at the clothes. Others ask you to look at the woman wearing them.
For The Face Winter 2025, Irina Shayk steps into a world where glamour feels both cinematic and strangely intimate. Photographed by Drew Vickers, the story strips away the excess often associated with modern fashion imagery, replacing it with sharp contrasts, lingerie-inspired silhouettes, and portraits that rely almost entirely on presence.
Rather than performing for the camera, Shayk allows each image to unfold with remarkable restraint. Every frame feels deliberate, balancing softness with confidence and sensuality with precision. It’s a reminder that the strongest fashion images rarely depend on elaborate sets, they depend on a model who understands exactly how to hold a photograph.
Drew Vickers’ photography amplifies that tension. His clean compositions and carefully controlled lighting create images that feel timeless rather than trend-driven, while Danielle Emerson’s styling references classic lingerie dressing without slipping into nostalgia. The result is an editorial that feels confident, modern, and quietly unforgettable.
Some editorials are remembered for the clothes. Others are remembered for the feeling they leave behind. This is one of them.
Publication: The Face Winter 2025/2026
Model: Irina Shayk
Photography: Drew Vickers
Styling: Danielle Emerson
Hair: Olivier Schawalder
Makeup: Yadim

posts.caption:
Fashion has always loved a great designer-muse relationship. But few have shaped pop culture quite like Donatella Versace and Lady Gaga.
When Lady Gaga became one of the biggest stars in the world, she didn’t just wear Versace, she embodied it.
At a time when Donatella was redefining the house after Gianni Versace’s legacy, Gaga brought the brand into a new cultural conversation. Suddenly, Versace wasn’t just synonymous with ‘90s supermodels and Italian glamour; it became the uniform of pop’s boldest provocateur.
Their relationship went far beyond celebrity dressing. Gaga regularly wore custom Versace on stage, at red carpets, and in editorials, while openly celebrating Donatella as both a designer and a friend. In return, Donatella embraced Gaga as one of the house’s defining muses. The bond became so close that Gaga even dedicated the song Donatella on ARTPOP to her which was transforming the designer into a genuine pop culture icon in her own right.
It’s difficult to imagine the 2010s without the two of them. Together, they helped redefine what Versace represented for a new generation. The brand became louder, bolder, unapologetically glamorous, and deeply connected to pop music and queer culture. While Versace had always been admired within fashion, its relationship with Lady Gaga introduced the house to an entirely new audience that embraced Donatella as more than a designer - she became a personality, a meme, an icon, and, for many, the glamorous mother figure of fashion.
Of course, Donatella Versace’s influence was never built by one person alone. She had already spent years shaping the house and proving herself as a designer. But Lady Gaga undeniably amplified that story. Their friendship blurred the lines between fashion and music in a way that felt authentic, creating one of the defining cultural partnerships of the decade.
Some collaborations sell clothes. This one built a legacy.

From Pierpaolo Piccioli’s first couture collection for @balenciaga, @dior’s highfalutin triangulation of Jonathan Anderson’s vision, Lynda Benglis’ art and the Christian Dior archive, and the excitement around Michael Stewart’s @standing_ground_ , Paris Haute Couture week was jam-packed with delights. In this week’s Big Style newsletter CNN’s @fiona.sinclair.scott sends a dispatch filled to the brim with fashion and culture from the French capital. If this sounds like something you want to read over your Saturday morning coffee, sign up now via the link in bio or by commenting Big Style.

posts.alt_text:
Last night, one of India's most influential tastemakers, @manishmalhotra05 made his Paris Haute Couture debut. Ahead of his show, he said he was less interested in presenting an Indian's take on couture, but rather he wanted to tell his story. The collection, sweetly titled "Maa," unfolds in four chapters, and is an ode to the woman who gave him life. The show came a couple of days after fellow Indian designer @rahulmishra_7 presented his collection of talismanic designs, deeply rooted in Indian sculptural traditions. Isha Ambani, a power player in the Indian fashion industry whose company owns a stake in both brands, attended the shows, telling CNN's Fiona Sinclair Scott, "the standards of couture are exacting, but Indian craftsmanship has met, and in many cases exceeded, these benchmarks for centuries."

For the full dispatch from Paris, subscribe to our Big Style newsletter, which hits inboxes on Saturdays. Tap the link in our bio or comment "Big Style" and we'll link you up.

📸: Courtesy Rahul Mishra

posts.caption:
"Have you seen @erling Haaland's custom Birkins?" TikToker Jack Savoie said in a video that quickly went viral. "Baby, buckle up."
"OK the first thing I should say is that they're not all Birkins," @thesavoiedaily said in a phone interview with CNN.
Many of Haaland's bags are actually Haut à Courroies (HAC), the model that the Birkin is based off, but with a slightly taller and narrower profile. That doesn't do anything to dull the impact of Haaland's collection, though.
The enviable collection has triggered another round of discussion about men and the innocent handbag, with photos of Haaland on the tarmac posted to social media riddled with homophobia and comments questioning the striker's masculinity.
The specific irony of something like an Hermès HAC being categorized as hyperfeminine is that the bag was created in the late 19th century as a carryall for horse riding equipment, much like the rest of the brand's offerings at the time.
The brand wouldn't introduce a handbag specifically marketed towards women until many decades later, when it expanded its offerings beyond leather goods. "I think there's just an increasing appreciation for beautiful things, gender aside," Savoie said.
"I don't even know why these bags have genders."
📸 : Aitor Alcalde/UEFA/Getty Images/Jason Howard/Bauer-Griffin/GC Images/David Jensen/

posts.caption:
@finnwolfhardofficial's upbringing was far from normal. ⁠
⁠
Catapulted to fame aged 13 as one of the stars of Stranger Things, his adolescent years were divided between high school in his hometown of Vancouver and long months in the US shooting the Netflix show. ⁠
⁠
Working so much throughout his childhood left little time for thinking about what he wanted to do with his life beyond the Upside Down, but since Stranger Things wrapped, he's been using music - another obsession of his, and one he pursued in teenage bands like Calpurnia and the Aubreys.⁠
⁠
Ahead of the release his sophomore album, Fire from the Hip, the musician and actor talks to Dazed about forging a new path, and why rock music should be funnier.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to read more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @fred_gs⁠
#FinnWolfhard #Dazed

posts.caption:
For decades, fine jewellery revolved around ruby, sapphire, and emerald.
Now, a new generation of coloured gemstones is creating fresh opportunities for the trade.
Spinels, tourmalines, and tsavorites are gaining momentum thanks to their vibrant colours, strong storytelling potential, and pricing flexibility.
These gemstones are not replacing the Big Three. They are expanding the palette of luxury.
For retailers and manufacturers, that means an opportunity to stand out with collections that feel distinctive, modern, and commercially smart.
The real winners will be brands that combine sourcing expertise, certification, and compelling storytelling to build trust and perceived value.
Which coloured gemstone category are you betting on for the future?
#DimondWorld #DWMagazine #ColouredGemstones #Spinel #Tourmaline

The diamond bourse isn’t disappearing. But it’s no longer the only room where deals get done. 💎
Nivoda: 10,000+ retailers. 70+ countries. Triple-digit growth. RapNet: 30 years. 100+ countries. Hundreds of millions traded annually. India: 3,500+ processing units. Still majority offline.
That gap is the biggest opportunity in B2B diamond trade right now.
This edition of #DiamondPulse breaks down why online marketplaces are no longer the future of diamond trading. They’re the present. The platforms, the proof, and exactly what to do about it.
Swipe. Save. Share with every trader you know.
#DiamondWorld #DWMagazine #DiamondOnline #DiamondTrading

posts.caption:
Is this a handbag… or high jewellery?
With 4,732 gemstones totalling 423 carats, this one-of-a-kind clutch by the late Manuel Rosas of Rosior proves that exceptional craftsmanship doesn’t have to stay inside a jewellery box.
Crafted in silver and gold and set with diamonds, rubies, emeralds, sapphires, and amethysts, it’s a remarkable example of how jewellery techniques can transform an everyday object into collectible art.
Some creations aren’t defined by what they are.
They’re defined by how they’re made.
Would your clients see this as a handbag, a work of art, or a piece of high jewellery? Tell us in the comments.
Video credits - @treasurehousefair
#DiamondWorld #BeyondJewellery #HighJewellery #LuxuryCollectibles #JewelleryDesign

posts.alt_text: GLOBAL (née LOCAL) designer Manish Malhotra has finally made his BIG Paris Haute Couture debut henny. Naturally, he arrived fully furnished with a bridal jhoomar and architectural silhouettes and more. Maximalists, this is your Super Bowl. The runway critics, historians, and couture professors who materialise every July. The floor (or the comment section) is yours. Thoughts, feelings, emotions. NOWWW. ✍🏽👨‍💻📝👀

posts.caption:
You know the Greek legend of Pygmalion, where a statue comes to life as a maiden? Well, at Rahul Mishra, the Indian version of the myth unfurled. Like as if by some couture divination, the carvings and statues, found in marvels like the Ajanta caves and Chennakesava Temple, took their first breaths and marched straight to sweltering hot Paris.
YES, the “Devi” archetype is once again visited. But the Ajanta has been around for 2000 years and the reference can never be used ad nauseam. Nobody reserves that right to be inspired by it! And here it has been refreshingly done in forms of trompe-l'œil, appliqués, embellishments, costume jewellery, and embroidery, that distanced itself from being on-the-nose theatrical and felt rather clever.
The sculptural headpieces are made by Sumant Kumar and the hats are by the icon Stephen Jones. The blinding gems around the models were provided by Tanishq, which marks yet another year of their partnership. Indian story, Indian designer, Indian jewels. Well, it does take a whole country to make a couture show at such calibre 😇😇😇

posts.caption: The Dior show was, well, can’t even say great-gowns, beautiful-gowns! Inspired by Indian tropical flora, this fern coat is giving me reheated nachos from RM’s first international couture show. But the kanjoosi in JWA’s appliqué is sending me to recession!! While RM’s was lush, forest fantasy, Dior is giving alopecia core. Maybe something to do with deforestation. 🫢😬👀😏🔪

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?
For ELLE’s Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build.
Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.
Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Illustration: Vikalp Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra)
Words by : Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
Cover Design: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar)
Editorial Co-ordinator: Sneh Lad (@sneh_lad)
[Pride, Queer Rights]

#ELLECoverStar: Victoria Harbour has been the face of Hong Kong for generations, where the city's soaring skyline meets the water in a view recognised around the world. It sets the stage for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her unmistakable presence to one of the city's most iconic locations. As the skyline shifts with the light, she reminds us that the most captivating moments don't always ask for attention. Sometimes, they’re simply felt. Read our interview with the cover star at the link in bio.
.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: Some actors play athletes, and athletes occasionally act. Saiyami Kher (@saiyami) belongs to a much smaller category. She genuinely understands both worlds. That made her movie Ghoomer particularly significant. Directed by R. Balki, the film follows a young cricketer rebuilding her life after a devastating accident. The role represented the convergence of two defining parts of her identity. The athlete helped her understand discipline and competition. The actor explored vulnerability, frustration and recovery. The result became one of the strongest performances of her career and earned widespread praise from audiences and athletes alike. Many para-athletes reached out to her after watching the film. What resonated with them was the authenticity of its portrayal. For Kher, that response mattered more than any statistic.
Read the full story at the 🔗 in bio.
On @saiyami:
Look 1: Che Ap jacket and Che Simp skirt, both by @ka_sha_india. Olive moonwing earrings and midi bloom cuff, both by @studiometallurgy
Look 2: H/W blue silk striped jacket by @akaaro. Lara dress by @urvashikaur. Twig necklace by @studiokassa. Miss Z heels by @christianlouboutin
Look 3: Shirt by @injiri
Look 4: Solana jacket and Solana pants, both by @anavila_m. Tribal turquoise necklace by @tribebyamrapali.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: The period following ‘Mirzya’ could easily have pushed Saiyami Kher (@saiyami) towards safer choices. The industry rewards familiarity, and actors often find themselves encouraged to fit a specific mould and stay there. Kher encountered those expectations early. “There is always an unspoken pressure to fit into this kind of mould. You have to look a certain way, behave a certain way, choose safer roles.” The pressure is particularly intense for women. So Kher decided she would rather build a career she recognised than one designed by somebody else. “I wouldn’t survive happily if I kept performing a version that’s not authentic to me.” That decision explains much about her filmography. Rather than chasing a singular image, Kher moved across languages, genres and platforms. Whether it was ‘Choked,’ ‘Special OPS,’ ‘Faadu’ or ‘8 A.M. Metro,’ she kept returning to women who were complicated rather than simply likeable.
Read the full story at the 🔗 in bio.
On @saiyami: Moon drop top and skirt, both by @chorusworld. Sacred songs earrings and Dawn ring, both by @zoyajewels. Reese yellow heels by @houseofprisca_.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: Cinema keeps Saiyami Kher (@saiyami) curious. Sport keeps her grounded. The space between the two has shaped one of Indian cinema’s most unconventional careers. For nearly a decade, Kher has quietly built a filmography that resists easy categorisation. She has moved between Hindi, Marathi, and Telugu cinema, between film and streaming, while simultaneously completing marathons, triathlons and Ironman races. Nothing about her journey has followed the obvious route. She comes from a film family, yet cinema barely featured in her childhood. She has the pedigree that often sparks assumptions about privilege, yet her story is one of auditions, rejection and persistence. Actors and athletes are often presented as two distinct identities. Kher has quietly made them one.
Read the full story at the 🔗 in bio.
On @saiyami: H/W blue silk striped jacket by @akaaro. Lara dress by @urvashikaur. Twig necklace by @studiokassa. Miss Z heels by @christianlouboutin

posts.caption:
Has Haute Couture Fashion Week ever been so copacetic as Fall ‘26? It seems that the designers have reached a point of consensus, so that each show was in harmony with the next.
Of course, The Jonatthieu Anderblazy of it all is hard to ignore. Though the similarities between Dior and Chanel this week became a point of conversation, each was elegant and whimsical, and offered up titillating nods to their house’s heritages; Chanel’s ultra blown-up tweed in look 47 and Dior’s ‘Arizona Coat’ in look 29, for example.
Other little (sometimes very little) details showed up numerous times through the week. Anyone notice the little tiny flower trend? In contrast to the bulging, decadent flowers we are accustomed to seeing on runways, this week’s florals were wild, pastoral, and lively. In the case of Schiaparelli, who opened the week, they were actually alive. One look used over ten thousand flowers and required almost three thousand hours of work.
Designers also leaned into  molded bodices in a myriad of eye-catching ways. Schiaparelli molded a bodice in glazed silicone which Daniel Roseberry described as an “augmented version of reality.” The effect was so powerful that the dress flew straight off the runway and onto Zendaya, who was in London for the world premiere of ‘The Odyssey.’ Ashi Studio showed a handful of bodices, many of which seemed, frankly, like fodder for 2026 cinema press tours. And Duran Latink, in his debut couture collection for Jean Paul Gaultier, used 3D printing to create a hyperrealistic distorted male torso with hand painted birthmarks and latex nipples.
Congratulations are in order for Pierpaolo Piccioli who showed his first couture collection for Balenciaga. By focusing on the dramatic silhouette of the house and eschewing stunts, he delivered that familiar airy refinement we came to expect from him at Valentino.
In all, this HCFW embraced innovation and history at the same time; Designers practiced using textures like silicon and plastic, they continued to utilize feathers in place of furs, and they pleated their little hearts out to create magnificent displays of craftsmanship. The future of haute couture seems bright.
posts.alt_text:
2026 Emmy noms are in! 

HBO shows ‘The Pitt’ leads with the most nominations, followed by ‘Hacks’ a record-breaking 24 nods, the most for a comedy series in a single season.

Spooky newcomer ‘Widows Bay’ lands in third with 19 nominations, followed by ‘Pluribus’ (both Apple TV originals).

Now that the list is out, which shows & actors do you think were snubbed?

#tv #emmy #actor #hollywood #news

posts.caption:
Looks like Armie Hammer’s return to Hollywood is already flailing. According to a new report from Puck, Hammer “was in tears” after watching his new film ‘Citizen Vigilante‘ and “not tears of joy.” An unnamed source from Hammer’s camp claims his reaction to the final cut was: “Fuck. This is hateful, disgusting.”
Written and directed by notorious filmmaker Uwe Boll, the movie follows a man who “takes justice into his own hands, hunting down criminals,” with those criminals primarily being immigrants.
The same insider said Hammer knew the film “certainly leaned toward the right,” but wasn’t prepared for how far it went, reportedly Hammer said, “that was not the movie I thought we made,” before he “freaked the fuck out.”
The film was considered so offensive that Germany initially blocked it from theaters over its anti-immigrant and anti-Muslim messaging, but in the US, it’s being praised by right-wing culture warriors. Elon Musk posted the full movie on X for two days, helping boost it to No. 1 on Apple and Amazon.
Boll embraced the MAGA fandom during a Reddit AMA last week. After a user referenced his past comments calling actors “whores” and asked if he was now “a whore yourself pleasing right wingers,” Boll replied: “Absolutely.”
Hammer, whose career tanked in 2021 after sexual assault allegations, denied the claims, and prosecutors ultimately declined to file charges. Since then, he’s struggled to land work, recently admitting to The Hollywood Reporter that he “would have done a f***ing cat food commercial.”
Over the weekend, Boll teased a potential sequel. After reportedly freaking out over the first one, will Hammer come back for round two or is a cat food commercial still his best bet?
#hollywood #movie #film #news #actor

posts.caption:
Taylor Swift married Travis Kelce on July 3rd at Madison Square Garden — We all know that. We also know that the bride wore Christian Dior Haute Couture Bridal by JW Anderson for the wedding. What we don’t know, however, is what the wedding gown looked like.
This morning—this afternoon, if you’re in Paris—Dior held their Fall 2026 Couture Show featuring, as is the custom for these shows, a wedding gown as the final look. It was a whimsical and mostly traditional off-the-shoulder gown of plissé white chiffon layered underneath white netting which was decorated with fern-shaped lace, feathery fern appliques, and powdery white flowers like dandelions. Trailing the dress was an asymmetrical train of the same material.
Being that Swift’s gown is one of the most anticipated pieces of bridal wear of the year (if not the decade) it’s almost impossible to imagine that Dior’s bridal look, shown just three days after the Swift/Kelce wedding, would have no relation or resemblance whatsoever to the gown Swift wore. Right?
Though details about the wedding are few, a source of The Hollywood Reporter described a luscious secret garden-esque environment which included “ferns and trees.” As well, AMC CEO Adam Aron revealed in now-deleted X posts that Swift wore a ”long white train” (like that seen on the runway today.) And, the flower appliqués on Dior’s wedding look are not dissimilar to the Queen Anne’s Lace seen on fragments of Swift’s bouquet which were posted by the guest who caught it during the toss.
All that said, Swift is notorious for dropping little hints and nods to her self-mythology. This is likely due to the voracious appetite of her fans who will dig into every detail of her lyrics and imagery for morsels of clarity about her inner life. Keeping her gown a secret seems to betray her career-long need to inflame speculation and conversation for as long as possible; Thus, the timing of Dior’s Couture Fall ‘26 show feels less-than coincidental as it provides yet another piece of the puzzle for fans to grab at.
Or, are we totally overthinking it? Is this just the Swiftie spell working its magic on us?

posts.caption:
Welp, America 250, a once-in-a-generation celebration, turned out to be the most on-brand shitshow for the Trump administration.
The nation’s semiquincentennial should have been an easy win for the president, especially since planning had been reportedly underway for nearly a decade. Organizers blamed the weather for the sparse crowds and canceled events, but the vibes were off way before the forecast.
Funding has come under scrutiny after House Dems alleged that some donors were unknowingly redirected from the bipartisan America 250 nonprofit to a different nonprofit promoting Trump-backed events. At least 10 states declined to send official delegations over concerns about the event’s messaging, including North Carolina, which instead ended up represented by a booth featuring images of the Confederate flag.
The weekend’s laundry list of L’s included masked white nationalists marching to “reclaim” America, MAGA supporters arguing with the National Guard after being ordered to evacuate for severe weather, and many taking refuge inside the National Museum of African American History and Culture (LOL, the irony). There were also baptisms on the National Mall?? Interesting choice for a nation founded on separating church and state.
As for entertainment, Vanilla Ice, the last recognizable headliner, bailed after weather delayed his set, leaving attendees with a band attempting to cover Beyoncé. Yes, Beyoncé. Later, members of the U.S. Armed Forces sang a rendition of Earth, Wind & Fire’s “September,” a song the band has previously objected to Trump using.
Saturday ended with what Trump called the “best fireworks show EVER,” promoted as a potential Guinness World Records contender. Instead, the display briefly gave D.C. the worst air quality of any major city on Earth per CNN, prompting a Code Red air quality alert.
Happy birthday, America?
#independenceday #fourthofjuly #dc #reels #news

posts.caption:
One moment, he was breaking through. The next, he was everywhere. Our July cover star, AP Dhillon (@apdhillon), has become one of the defining voices of a generation. Through the rise, the noise and the spotlight, he’s remained focused on what matters most: the music. In our latest cover story, he opens up about balancing the artist the world knows with the person he’s always been.
Chairperson: Avarna Jain (@avarna_jain)
CEO: Debashish Ghosh
Editor: Rahul Gangwani (@rahulgangs_)
Creative Direction and Cover Story: Komal Shetty (@komal_shetty_)
Photography: Taras Taraporvala (@taras84)
Styling: Nikita Jaisinghani (@nikitajaisinghani)
Cover Design: Azad Mohan
Bookings Editor: Varun Shah (@varunnshah)
Talent Liaison: Mélange Digital (@melangedigital.co)
Producer: Manju Balakrishnan (@manju_b)
Production house: Popcorn Productions (@popcorn_mb)
Location: Ng Teng Fong Roof Garden, National Gallery (@nationalgallerysingapore)
Destination Partner: Singapore Tourism Board (@visit_singapore)
On AP
Denim Jacket by Alexander McQueen (@alexandermcqueen) from Galeries Lafayette (@galerieslafayetteindia); Studded Denim Jeans by Valentino (@maisonvalentino); Shoes by Bottega Veneta (@newbottega); Rings by Amrapali (@amrapalijewels)
An @rpsg.lifestylemedia publication by @rpsggroup.
#EsquireIndia #APDhillon #VisitSingapore
posts.alt_text:
He may be AP (@apdhillon) to the world, but he’s also Amritpal Singh Dhillon. The man who still goes to the same barber, skips a hair and grooming team because he believes imperfection is real, and often forgets just how famous he is. Fame brought him freedom, financial stability, and love from fans that money can’t buy—but it also came at a cost. Our July cover star AP Dhillon (@apdhillon) opens up about life beyond the spotlight.

posts.caption:
When Sahil Sharma chose the stage name ‘Zaeden’, he didn’t realise that his final initial would automatically place him at the bottom of every poster. Naturally, he had to work his way up to becoming a headliner for festivals. While that happened, he would become one of the youngest DJs to be featured on the Dutch radio show Hardwell On Air—at 19. Just over a decade on, Zaeden has found a new rhythm to groove to—acting. He’s arrived at our shoot after filming 14 hours straight from his first acting project.
We caught up with the musician to decode his choices, his personal style and what it means to evolve beyond the stage. Read the full story at the link in bio.
Words by: Rudra Mulmule (@poetryandpastaa)
Photographer: Karthik Shetty (@theshettykarthik)
Styling: Komal Shetty (@komal_shetty_)
Hair Styling: Vikram Jahagirdar (@vikram_aj85) From Team Darshan Yewalekar (@darshanyewalekar)
Agency: XI Artists (@xiartists)
Grooming: Prajakta Sawant (@makeupbyprajakta6)
Bookings & Production: Varun Shah (@varunnshah)
Location Courtesy: Paashh, India (@paashh.india)
Look 1: Jacket by AllSaints (@allsaints), customised by Zaeden. Shirt by Bianca Jeswant (@biancajeswantclothing). Jeans by Zara (@zara). Rolex Datejust watch with the Wimbledon dial by Rolex (@rolex). Airforce 1 sneakers by Nike (@nike).
Look 2: Tank and jeans by Bershka (@bershka). Bomber jacket and sneakers by AMIRI (@amiri). Sunglasses by Armani Exchange (@armaniexchange).
Look 3: Shirt by Nicobar (@nicojournal). Chinos by Bombay Shirt Company (@bombayshirts). Rolex Datejust watch with the Wimbledon dial by Rolex (@rolex). Airforce 1 sneakers by Nike (@nike).
#EsquireIndia #3Fits

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
As Elle Woods herself would be pleased to inform you, fashion insiders have anointed pink one of the season’s hottest shades.

Sightings amongst the spring/summer 2026 runways of at @maisonalaïa, @balenciaga,@chanelofficial, and more have led us to proclaim blush pink “this season’s rising color du jour.” Even the World Cup is getting in on the trend: Soccer players are sporting a surprising amount of “saturated, unapologetic pink.” And perhaps now more than ever, the ‘Legally Blonde’ lead, bright and brilliant as she is, feels like a fitting fashion reference 25 years on.

Tap the link in bio to learn more about this season’s biggest color trend—and comment “pink” to see how you can embrace the hue, Elle Woods-style.

posts.caption: It was an action-packed—and sweltering—couture week, where body-sculpting silhouettes, exacting tailoring, and the natural world shaped the conversation. Amid the ongoing press tour for #TheOdyssey, goddess dresses were aplenty, seen at fashion-favorite label @Standing_ground and in Maria Grazia Chiuri’s debut @Fendi couture show in Rome. At @chanel, Matthieu Blazy reworked house signatures—from tweed to the LBD—while Jonathan Anderson showed fantastical Bar jackets wrapped in leaf-green fringe and soft ruffles for his second @Dior couture offering. Here, take in the top five trends from the couture runways.

posts.caption:
Gabrielle Chanel’s immortal advice—to remove one last thing before leaving the house—has shaped generations of dressers. @matthieu_blazy’s spring/summer 2026 @chanelofficial couture collection, unveiled earlier this week, proposed an enticing alternative: leave it all on...and then add another layer for good measure.
From the fairy-tale sheers and surreal textures at @chanelofficial to @dior’s pleated pannier dresses, designers appear to have reached peak quiet luxury fatigue. The mood has carried over to jewelry, where piled-on necklaces have replaced minimal colliers and barely-there chains. Stuck on which necklace to wear? The answer, according to the runways, is all of them.
Comment “necklace” to see how you can embrace high fashion’s maximalist jewelry sensibility.

posts.caption:
Four weeks later and we’re finally here: at the quarterfinals of the #WorldCup. What began as 40-plus teams vying for the title of world champions has has come down to eight teams still dreaming of winning the whole thing at the July 19 final: France, Morocco, Spain, Belgium, Norway, England, Argentina, and Switzerland.
But while we make it through these last days, games, and teams, there’s still ample opportunity to see the sights across the host countries of the United States, Canada, and Mexico. Seize every remaining opportunity with our guide to the best places to visit during the quarterfinals—at the link in bio.

posts.caption: My outfit definitely expresses how I’m feeling. I might get extra done up if I’m feeling bad or in a funk and I don’t feel good when I’m not put together. Also I love style and fashion it’s like art for me putting outfits together. But there are many people that just get dressed and don’t think about it one way or another so I’m not sure that it affects everyone in the same way.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.alt_text: The tea on LV v Molly Tea

posts.caption: I’m all for protecting one’s design and logo but this seems like a stretch. Like no one is going to think of LV when they look at Molly Tea’s pink cup… And the Chinese are so right. Just because we didn’t have the concept of “trademark” back in the day, they now have to pay a European luxury brand to use elements of their own cultural heritage 👀 like what world are we living in??
posts.alt_text: comment ‘writing’ to receive more details and reserve your spot

posts.caption: It absolutely is a trademark infringement, made more certain by the 7/11 date the sneaker is to drop in stores. However, I would handle the situation differently. I would ask for the same restitution in my complaint demand but I would have included a collaborative clause /ADR in my lawsuit demand letter, offering a "pre-suit mediation" provision allowing for us to enter into a brand collaboration/ licensing agreement once the other terms have been satisfied. This way as a plaintiff you set yourself up from the outset as “resolution-focused” over protracted litigation. This gives you a leg up over the defense as being a more responsible party and it will resonate with a judge / jury.

posts.caption:
For nearly two decades, our cover star, Yami Gautam Dhar, has built a career on one simple principle: never be predictable. From romantic dramas to thrillers, from characters who quietly endure to women who command the narrative, she has consistently chosen reinvention over repetition. Thoughtful, fearless, and deeply committed to her craft, Yami continues to challenge both herself and her audience with every role she takes on. Just a few reasons why we stan!

posts.caption:
“You know, there comes a time at a very crucial point in your life when you become absolutely fearless and just want to work without having anything else on your mind. The only thing I think about is whether I will love doing what I’ve just committed to. Of course, you want the outcome to be proportionate to your passion, but I also know it is not in my hands. What is in my control is to make sure that whatever I say ‘yes’ to, I’m happy to do, to enjoy it thoroughly.”

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
Are your clothes helping or harming the issue of water waste? 🌊⁠
⁠
In a new paper from @fashionrevolutionjapan, the issue is explored at length, revealing solutions and problems around fashion and its use of water. Of particular interest are four exciting innovations that prevent water waste and seek to keep water clean, all of which help to protect environments, animals, and communities too 💙⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about four fibre innovations that are focused on treating water as the precious resource that it is. Visit the link to read the full report ↑⁠
⁠
#Water #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #FashionRevolution #FashionLovers

posts.caption:
Don’t fall for Zara’s flowy pants. Why? They’re a hazard to your health 😵⁠
⁠
Countless videos of persons wearing a pair of polyester “flowy” pants by Zara are going viral as the pants are so voluminous that they are causing people to trip, largely as they get caught up in people’s shoes.⁠
⁠
This is a layered issue but it does a great job of representing fast fashion’s low values: they’d rather rush a trending product that might harm people instead of taking the time to quality control a garment. If they treat you—the consumer—this way, how are they treating their workers? The planet? Animals? We unfortunately know the answer all too well 😤⁠
⁠
Swipe to see explore this latest Zara drama. What are your thoughts on this issue? If you’re looking for summer pants like this, opt out of fashion and support smaller, more sustainable brands: find a few ideas at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Zara #ZaraWoman #ZaraPants #ZaraTrousers #Fashion⁠

posts.caption:
This NAIDOC week, we wanted to celebrate Indigenous communities worldwide 🗺️⁠
⁠
As this effort to highlight the aboriginal, islander, and larger Indigenous communities of Australia celebrates its 50th occasion, this is a reminder of the resilience and strength of the community. It would serve the wider fashion industry well to take note of this too, considering it is a business built upon colonial practices and exploitation of local (and often Indigenous) communities.⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about four “Good” and “Great” Indigenous brands from around the world making clothing and accessories ⏩ Which Indigenous brands would you like to see rated? Share in the comments, then visit the link to explore more rated Indigenous brands 🔗⁠
⁠
#NAIDOC2026 #50YearsOfNAIDOC #Fashion #Indigenous #IndigenousFashion⁠

posts.caption:
For @shakirakhan16, appearing on #LoveIsland as a South Asian woman was not a responsibility she took lightly. ‘I vividly remember the nerves I felt,’ she writes for Grazia. ‘I was about to enter the public eye on a whole new scale, representing a diverse and resilient community of women who are rarely given the platform they deserve.’
Now, she wants to highlight the specific issues women in her community face, and is fronting Refuge’s new campaign to tackle domestic abuse. In her profoundly personal piece at the link in the bio, Khan says South Asian women face specific pressures when it comes to accessing support.
‘Instead of being appreciated as an act of incredible courage, leaving a partner – even an abusive one – is still viewed as shameful or dishonourable in many communities,’ Khan explains. ‘Ultimately, South Asian women are taught to stay silent, no matter the risk.’
TAP the link in the bio to read more and help support Refuge’s Summer Appeal.

posts.caption:
Would you turn down a wedding simply because you can’t afford to go?
With the average guest spending £316 just to attend a wedding, saying ‘yes’ to every invitation is becoming increasingly unrealistic. Particularly in your thirties, when peak wedding season can mean three or more celebrations crammed into the summer. New research from Tesco Bank found that 31% of us have RSVP’d ‘no’ to a wedding, because we can’t afford it, rising to 48% of Gen Z and 43% for Millennials.
At the link in bio @pollyvernon explores the growing cost of wedding season. ‘It’s my experience people can take their weddings Terribly Seriously, which makes you’re not going, tantamount to An Unforgivable Snub’ she writes ‘But needs must, cash costs, decisions must be made  - and longstanding friendships, inevitably disrupted.’
Tap the link in bio to read more.

posts.caption:
Iconic bookworm #DuaLipa has announced she’s opening the ‘Manifesto Library’ in Porto – stocking books that ‘ask questions’ and some that have been banned by school districts for themes of sexuality or race. ‘This library is a shrine to books that have disappeared, to authors whose courage unmasks structures of power and control, and to readers who refuse to be told what book they are allowed to read,’ she says.
Subversive literature for free thinkers? Sounds hot. Indeed, the latest data suggests reading is increasingly attractive, with mentions of books in dating bios up 29% overall, and up 41% for women, according to Tinder. This summer, let’s booksmax, not looksmax.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana

Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind

posts.caption:
Paris has a new coffee destination, and it's dressed in @ysl. ⁠
⁠
The fashion house has officially opened a café in its new Avenue Montaigne boutique. The café will serve everything from coffee, matcha, and pastries, but it's the interior that speaks to Saint Laurent's signature vision. ⁠
⁠
Also featured are sleek interiors, vibrant furniture, a terrace, and even a library with a curated selection of reads. Swipe for a closer look.⁠
⁠
📷️: @twoarabminds
posts.alt_text:
There’s something big happening in the world of folding fans. ⁠
⁠
@porter_yoshida_co.official, Yoshida & Co.’s flagship brand of military-infused bags, has quietly restocked its own elevated take on the folding fan it created in collaboration with Kyoto’s historic fan specialty shop Miyawaki Baisenan.⁠
⁠
The PORTER iteration is crafted from traditional bamboo and Japanese paper, scented with musk, and stamped with both the brand logo and Yoshida & Co.’s kawaii little Porter-kun mascot; it’s accessorized with a sleek braided-cord wrist strap and comes enclosed in a carrying case crafted from uber-durable PORTER nylon twill. Swipe for a closer look.
